source.title: Improved betulinic acid biosynthesis using synthetic yeast chromosome recombination and semi-automated rapid LC-MS screening

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: The paper addresses the bottleneck in screening large strain libraries generated by SCRaMbLE for improved biosynthesis of metabolites that lack easily selectable phenotypes, such as betulinic acid (BA). The goal is to develop a semi-automated workflow combining rapid LC-MS and nanopore sequencing to quickly identify and characterize improved strains.

section_gists.heading: Improved betulinic acid biosynthesis using synthetic yeast chromosome recombination and semi-automated rapid LC-MS screening
section_gists.gist: Introduces SCRaMbLE as a powerful tool for diversifying yeast genomes but limited by screening throughput for non-colorimetric metabolites. Proposes a workflow using ultra-fast LC-MS (84 s per sample) and nanopore sequencing to screen 1000 colonies in 24 h. Demonstrates with betulinic acid production, achieving 2- to 7-fold titre improvements in 12 strains.
process_signals: SCRaMbLE generates improved strains at ~1% rate, necessitating high-throughput screening.
Previous studies used selectable or colorful phenotypes; this work targets metabolites only detectable by LC-MS.
Ultra-fast LC-MS adapted from therapeutic drug monitoring reduces per-sample time from ~5 min to 84 s.
methods: SCRaMbLE of synthetic chromosome V with 176 loxPsym sites
Ultra-fast LC-MS using guard column (72 s runtime)
failures_or_limits: Standard LC-MS takes ~5 min per sample, limiting screening scale.

section_gists.heading: Results
section_gists.gist: Describes construction of BA-producing strain yGG066 (0.28 μg/gCDW). SCRaMbLE induced, 964 colonies screened. Ultra-fast LC-MS method validated with robustness and minimal drift. 12 strains exceeded 4σ threshold; all grew slower. Top strain BC11 had 2.3-fold improvement. Nanopore sequencing revealed BC11 had a deletion of TIR1 3' UTR, confirmed by PCR. qPCR showed TIR1 downregulation and PRE1 upregulation. Recapitulation of deletion in parental strain increased BA titre 2.4-fold, validating genotype-phenotype link.
process_signals: Initial BA titre was low (0.28 μg/gCDW).
Ultra-fast LC-MS maintained ion separation and robustness over 11 plates.
Hit threshold set at 4σ above control mean.
All 12 hits grew slower than controls.
BC11 deletion of TIR1 3' UTR was the only SCRaMbLE event.
methods: Strain construction: yGG066 with synV, AtLUS1, AtATR1, BPLO, tHMG1, ERG9
SCRaMbLE induction with beta-estradiol for 4 h
failures_or_limits: All improved strains exhibited slower growth.
Only one SCRaMbLE event detected in BC11; other strains not fully characterized.
Low initial titre may limit detection sensitivity.

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: Highlights workflow's throughput advantage without compromising robustness. Notes that black-box methods like SCRaMbLE can reveal non-obvious strategies, e.g., TIR1 downregulation improving BA titre possibly via cell wall changes. Workflow applicable to other genotype diversification methods. Limitations include need for further automation and cost reduction for broader use.
process_signals: Workflow designed for post-SCRaMbLE screening where only 1% of colonies improve.
Non-obvious finding: small non-coding deletion affecting TIR1 and PRE1 expression boosts BA.
Suggests BA accumulation at cell wall; TIR1 downregulation may alter cell wall structure.
failures_or_limits: Throughput still limited by LC-MS; further automation needed.
Cost of nanopore sequencing may be prohibitive for larger screens.
Scalability to industrial strains not demonstrated.

section_gists.heading: Methods
section_gists.gist: Details strain construction (yGG066), SCRaMbLE protocol, automated colony picking and extraction, ultra-fast LC-MS parameters, CRISPR knockout for validation, and nucleic acid isolation for sequencing and qPCR.
process_signals: Strain construction used MoClo Yeast ToolKit.
SCRaMbLE induced with 1 μM beta-estradiol for 4 h.
Automated extraction reduced time to <30 min per plate.
Ultra-fast LC-MS used guard column with 72 s gradient.
CRISPR knockout used to validate TIR1 deletion effect.
methods: Automated extraction: CyBio FeliX robot, IPA extraction
Ultra-fast LC-MS: Agilent 1290 LC, 6550 Q-ToF, guard column
CRISPR knockout: cas9 plasmid pWS2082, gRNA plasmid gGG002

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: SCRaMbLE can generate genetic diversity that improves heterologous metabolite production, but screening throughput is a major bottleneck.
Ultra-fast LC-MS can enable screening of 1000 strains in a day, making SCRaMbLE practical for non-colorimetric products.
Combining automated sample preparation, rapid LC-MS, and nanopore sequencing creates a complete workflow from diversification to genotype-phenotype validation.
Non-obvious genetic changes (e.g., non-coding deletions) can significantly improve titre and inform rational engineering.
uncertainties_to_resolve: What is the full diversity of SCRaMbLE events in the other 11 improved strains?
Can the workflow be scaled to screen tens of thousands of strains?
Is the TIR1-PRE1 mechanism generalizable to other triterpenoids or pathways?
What are the long-term stability and scalability of the improved strains in industrial conditions?
Can the ultra-fast LC-MS method be adapted for other metabolites with similar throughput?

events.summary: SCRaMbLE generates improved strains at ~1% rate, but screening is limited for metabolites without selectable phenotypes.
Standard LC-MS takes ~5 min per sample, limiting screening of large strain libraries.
Workflow combines automated sample preparation, ultra-fast LC-MS, and barcoded nanopore sequencing to rapidly isolate and characterize improved strains.
SCRaMbLE induced for 4 h with 1 μM beta-estradiol, then 964 colonies screened by ultra-fast LC-MS.
12 strains exceeded 4σ threshold above control mean for BA titre improvement.
Re-analysis confirmed 12 strains had 2- to 7-fold higher BA titres than control.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers problem statement, workflow design, strain construction, SCRaMbLE induction, screening, and initial validation of hits.

events.summary: Sequencing of top strain BC03 revealed four SCRaMbLE rearrangements on synthetic chromosome V.
CRISPR-based genome engineering recreated the TIR1 3'UTR deletion in control strain yGG066, confirming it as the cause of improved BA titre.
No obvious rearrangement was detected by sequencing for three of the top 12 strains, suggesting natural mutations elsewhere may have caused improvements.
Hypothesis that BA accumulates at the cell wall and changes to cell wall structure may affect BA accumulation, explaining TIR1 deletion effect.
Ultra-fast LC-MS guard column method allowed sample characterisation every 84 s, fourfold faster than standard column.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains key validation and analysis events linking genotype to phenotype, and discusses workflow design and limitations.

events.summary: Ultra-fast LC-MS method designed using a guard column and 72 s gradient for rapid BA detection.
methods_or_tools: Zorbax Eclipse Plus C18 UHPLC guard column

events.summary: CRISPR knockout used to recapitulate TIR1 3' UTR deletion in parental strain yGG066.
Nanopore sequencing performed on 12 improved strains (BC01-12) to identify SCRaMbLE events.
Sequence analysis pipeline used to detect SCRaMbLE events by aligning reads to pre-SCRaMbLE synV and searching for LoxPsym boundaries.
qPCR performed to measure expression of TIR1 and PRE1 in BC11 and parental strain.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk contains detailed methods for ultra-fast LC-MS, CRISPR knockout, nanopore sequencing, sequence analysis, qPCR, and automated sample preparation, all directly supporting the paper's workflow.

evidence_bank.quote: SCRaMbLE generates improved strains at a rate of approximately one in a hundred
the time required for each sample is significant, limiting its use for screening large strain libraries
we combine automated sample preparation, an ultra-fast 84-second LC-MS method, and barcoded nanopore sequencing to rapidly isolate and characterise the best performing strains
964 induced colonies were picked
A threshold for significant titre improvement was set 4 standard deviations above the mean control values
A total of 12 screened strains exceeded this threshold
All 12 strains did indeed show significantly higher BA titres of between two- and sevenfold above that of the yGG066 control
Sequencing of this strain revealed a complex combination of four SCRaMbLE rearrangements on synthetic chromosome V.
Two non-coding deletions of 551 and 391 bp, respectively, were seen alongside a 10192 bp inversion and 8724 bp duplication
we used CRISPR-based genome engineering to artificially re-create this deletion in the control yGG066 strain, removing the TIR1 3'UTR region
We observe the same BA titre as in BC11, confirming that this single SCRaMbLE event is responsible for the threefold increase in BA biosynthesis.
no obvious rearrangement was detected by our sequencing approach for three of the strains.
We suspect that BA accumulates at the cell wall and therefore any changes to the cell wall structure may affect the accumulation of BA.
the ultrafast LC-MS guard column method, which allowed us to characterise samples every 84 s, fourfold faster than when using a standard chromatographic column.
A Zorbax Eclipse Plus C18 UHPLC guard column (2.1 × 5 mm, 1.8 μm particle size; Agilent Technologies, Santa Clara, CA, USA) was used for analysis.
The LC gradient was 72 s in total without post-time run as summarised in Supplementary Data 1.
Donor DNA was generated by PCR amplification of the dTIR1-3' region of BC11 using primers GG126 (GTATTCCTCACGATTAGAACCAGCC) and GG127 (CGTAAAGATTT CGCTGCGAAAG).
Extracted DNA from BC01-12 was prepared for Oxford Nanopore sequencing using genomic DNA kit SQK-LSK109 with 1D Native Barcoding kit EXP-NDB104 (Oxford Nanopore Technologies).
Sequencing was run for 48 h with local basecalling in MinKNOW.
Reads were demultiplexed using Porechop (https://github.com/rrwick/Porechop) and aligned to pre-SCRaMbLE synV sequence using LASTAL (-1100 flag) (http://last.cbrc.jp/).
qPCR was performed in a MasterCycler ep RealPlex 4 (Eppendorf) using a SYBR Green JumpStart qPCR kit.
Primers were designed using the IDT RealTime PCR Design Tool (http://idtdna.com/scitools/applications/realtimepcr).

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Screening large strain libraries generated by SCRaMbLE for improved biosynthesis of metabolites like betulinic acid is bottlenecked by the low throughput of standard LC-MS, which takes ~5 min per sample, making it impractical to find rare (~1%) improved strains.

canonical_events.summary: SCRaMbLE generates improved strains at ~1% rate, but screening is limited for metabolites without selectable phenotypes.
Standard LC-MS takes ~5 min per sample, limiting screening of large strain libraries.
Workflow combines automated sample preparation, ultra-fast LC-MS (84 s per sample), and barcoded nanopore sequencing to rapidly isolate and characterize improved strains.
Ultra-fast LC-MS method designed using a guard column and 72 s gradient for rapid BA detection.
key_entities: Zorbax Eclipse Plus C18 UHPLC guard column

canonical_events.summary: SCRaMbLE induced for 4 h with 1 μM beta-estradiol, then 964 colonies screened by ultra-fast LC-MS.
12 strains exceeded 4σ threshold above control mean for BA titre improvement.
Re-analysis confirmed 12 strains had 2- to 7-fold higher BA titres than control.
Nanopore sequencing performed on 12 improved strains (BC01-12) to identify SCRaMbLE events.
Sequence analysis pipeline used to detect SCRaMbLE events by aligning reads to pre-SCRaMbLE synV and searching for LoxPsym boundaries.
Sequencing of top strain BC03 revealed four SCRaMbLE rearrangements on synthetic chromosome V.
qPCR performed to measure expression of TIR1 and PRE1 in BC11 and parental strain.
CRISPR knockout used to recapitulate TIR1 3' UTR deletion in parental strain yGG066.
CRISPR-based genome engineering recreated the TIR1 3'UTR deletion in control strain yGG066, confirming it as the cause of improved BA titre.
Hypothesis that BA accumulates at the cell wall and changes to cell wall structure may affect BA accumulation, explaining TIR1 deletion effect.
No obvious rearrangement was detected by sequencing for three of the top 12 strains, suggesting natural mutations elsewhere may have caused improvements.

edges.reason: Both are problems that motivate the need for a high-throughput screening method.
The problem of slow LC-MS leads to the design of a faster workflow.
The ultra-fast LC-MS method is a specific implementation of the workflow design.
The ultra-fast LC-MS method is used in the SCRaMbLE screening experiment.
Screening yields data that is analyzed to identify hits.
Re-analysis confirms the initial hit identification.
Confirmed hits are sequenced to identify genetic changes.
Sequencing data is processed through a pipeline to detect SCRaMbLE events.
The pipeline identifies specific rearrangements in BC03.
After identifying a deletion in TIR1, qPCR is used to measure expression changes.
Expression changes motivate recapitulation of the deletion via CRISPR.
CRISPR recapitulation confirms the genotype-phenotype link.
Validation leads to a hypothesis about the mechanism.
Sequencing fails to find rearrangements in three strains, indicating a limitation.
